segments.text: [Speaker Notes] Chapter 9, Opener, Complex polysaccharides in bacterial cell walls, the site of action of antibiotics. The cell surface of Gram-positive bacteria consists of a lipid bilayer membrane surrounded by complex polymers of sugars (abbreviated here as NAM and NAG) crosslinked by short peptide chains. Penicillin inhibits the crosslinking reaction. This causes weakening of the cell wall, which, coupled with continued growth of the bacteria, makes it impossible for cells to resist internal pressure, and eventually they rupture.
